summary: Shaw proposes a framework for categorizing paintings along two axes: liberal vs. conservative drawing, and liberal vs. conservative painting (color/application). He observes that American Impressionism typically features conservative (accurate) drawing with liberal (sloppy) painting, which he sees as a safe cliché. He maps out four quadrants — from hyperrealism (conservative/conservative) to abstract expressionism (liberal/liberal) — and plans a systematic painting project exploring all four dimensions using the same subject across four canvases.
key_points: American Impressionism can be characterized as 'correctly drawn, sloppily painted' — a safe formula that lets artists prove technical skill while appearing expressive
Paintings can be categorized on two independent axes: how liberally or conservatively the drawing is done, and how liberally or conservatively the paint is applied

Each quadrant maps to recognizable art movements: hyperrealism (conservative/conservative), cubism (liberal/conservative), American Impressionism (conservative/liberal), abstract expressionism (liberal/liberal)
Shaw plans a methodical project painting the same subject four times, once in each quadrant, to explore how changing one dimension at a time affects the result
money_quotes: What Americans seem to like is a form of impressionism that is 'correctly drawn, sloppily painted'.

He took a kleenex and flopped it around on the canvas to fuck it up. It was if he couldn't bring himself to paint it sloppy but knew that a sloppy painting with correct proportions and perspective would sell well.
It's a safe cliché that lets you be conservative and prove you can paint correctly, but you just decide not to because you're totally a liberal artist libertine type dude.
I've experienced where if I just paint at random and pretend I'm letting go that people seem to think I'm crafting genius when it's just random bullshit.
I think it was Jackson Pollock who said you don't walk into a fucking rose garden and ask what it means. You just go in there because it's pretty and you like it.

It's yet another cliché to slap paint on a canvas randomly and then declare it a statement about the plight of the poor while you sell it for hundreds of thousands of dollars to rich asshole who will lock it in his million dollar apartment.
If you get the values and drawing right you can do anything with color.
key_insight: The tension between technical accuracy and expressive freedom in painting mirrors a broader cultural dynamic, and systematically exploring both axes can reveal which combinations produce genuinely interesting art versus comfortable clichés.
